As events turned out, Clare failed to obtain a victory in this match, but we are anticipating. In Search of a Row. "Will there be a row," was the question which framed itself on many lips. Why should there be a row? This is the question of diversion, thank Heaven, has not yet become an essential addition to important hurling events. Let us examine the circumstances which gave rise to the query. Because Limerick were worried by Cork, those of little faith and lack of knowledge of the motives by which genuine Gaels are evidently anticipated that the Limerick lads were only awaiting this opportunity of falling on the Cork hurlers and exterminating them to the last bootlace. These general and Limerick Markets Field figures in history as a Gaelic Armageddon, in which the hurling ball was forgotten and all bands devoted themselves to the crackling of skulls and the gentle pursuit of wallowing in gore. This was a serious reflection upon the Gaels of Limerick, and right nobly did they throw the insult back in the teeth of the narrow-minded pessimists whose theoretical hatreds propounded bloodshed. The Limerick County Board saw to it that the playing-pitch was kept inviolate, and particular praise must be given to Mr. John Kelly. In a considerable experience of the methods adopted to properly have a large crowd, and to see anything better, nor any body of stewards more considerate, more resolute or more successful in achieving the object aimed at.

A Great Muster. It was a great muster. The Markets Field was never filled to such capacity before, and the crowd probably created a Munster record. The spectacle of virile Irish manhood here presented would have rejoiced the hearts of the spectators, and the support was fairly even divided. The chances of a win for Cork were favourably canvassed before the match, and if the terms of the betting market were fulfilled upon them, they were farm favourites. Both teams took the field looking perfectly fit and capable, and the whole thirty would have scaled a singularly light average weight. What little advantage these in bulk rain fell at the commencement of hostilities, but the weather held up well subsequently. The teams were:

Clare-J. Nersay (goal), J. Shalke, J. Grace, T. McGrath, T. Deber, J. Guerin, A. Callan, J. Clancy, W. Connolly, M. Moloney, J. Spillacy, A. Power (capt.), J. Power, P. Hannon, M. Rogers.

Cork-Shalke (goal), F. Buckley, T. O'Leary, W. Walsh, M. Byrne (safed), B. Murphy (clough), L. F. P. Murphy, S. Murphy, J. F. F. O'Flaherty, J. H. O'Flaherty, J. Murphy (St. Finbar's).

The Game in Paragraphs. The teams were elected to play against the city goal. R. O'Leary and B. Walsh put them upon the aggressive immediately, and from Walsh's drive
